title: TCP/IP Model: The Four-Layer Reality
description: Compare the OSI reference model with the actual TCP/IP stack that runs the internet.

slides.title: OSI vs. TCP/IP: Theory vs. Reality 📚
slides.content:
# The OSI Model is Beautiful, but TCP/IP Rules the World

In Chapter 2, we learned the **OSI 7-Layer Model**. It's the perfect theoretical framework.

But the actual **Internet Protocol Suite** (commonly called **TCP/IP**) was developed by the **Department of Defense (DoD)** *before* the OSI model was finalized.

**Key Difference:**
- **OSI:** 7 Layers (Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical).
- **TCP/IP:** 4 Layers (Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access).

**Analogy:** OSI is like the detailed **blueprint** of a car engine (every gasket and bolt). TCP/IP is the **actual car** you drive to work. Some of the blueprint details are just welded together into bigger components in the real thing.

slides.title: The TCP/IP Four-Layer Stack 🥞
slides.content:
# Mapping the Models

| TCP/IP Layer | Corresponding OSI Layers | Key Protocols |
|--------------|--------------------------|---------------|
| **Application** | Application, Presentation, Session (5-7) | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, DHCP |
| **Transport** | Transport (4) | **TCP**, **UDP** |
| **Internet** | Network (3) | **IP**, **ICMP**, **ARP** (sort of) |
| **Network Access** | Data Link, Physical (1-2) | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP |

**The Big Shift:** TCP/IP doesn't have separate Session and Presentation layers. Those functions (encryption, formatting, session management) are **baked into the Application layer protocols themselves**.

**Example:** HTTPS handles encryption (Presentation) and cookie management (Session) directly within the application protocol, without relying on separate OSI layers.

quizData.question: How many layers are in the TCP/IP model?
quizData.explanation: Correct! The TCP/IP model has 4 layers: Application, Transport, Internet, and Network Access.

slides.title: Encapsulation in TCP/IP 📦
slides.content:
# Same Concept, Different Names

Data still gets wrapped in headers as it goes down the stack.

**TCP/IP Terminology:**

| Layer | Data Unit Name |
|-------|----------------|
| Application | **Message / Data** |
| Transport (TCP/UDP) | **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP) |
| Internet (IP) | **Packet** |
| Network Access (Ethernet) | **Frame** |

**Remember:** At the Transport layer, a **TCP Segment** is encapsulated inside an **IP Packet**, which is encapsulated inside an **Ethernet Frame**.

slides.title: The 'Hourglass' Shape ⌛
slides.content:
## The Narrow Waist of the Internet

The TCP/IP protocol stack is often drawn as an **hourglass**.

- **Top (Application):** Wide variety of apps (Web, Email, Gaming).
- **Middle (IP):** **NARROW WAIST**. **Every single application** must use **IP** to get anywhere. There is no alternative. This universal adoption of IP is the single greatest reason the internet scales globally.
- **Bottom (Network Access):** Wide variety of physical links (Fiber, 5G, Ethernet).

**Cybersecurity Implication:** Because **IP** is the universal language, attackers can craft malicious IP packets that work over *any* physical connection. An attack designed in the 90s still works today over Wi-Fi because the IP layer hasn't changed fundamentally.

slides.title: Visualizing the Stack 🖼️
slides.content:
Data flow down the TCP/IP stack:

```
Application:  "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1"
   |
Transport:    [TCP Header | HTTP Request Data]
   |
Internet:     [IP Header | TCP Header | HTTP Request Data]
   |
Network Acc:  [Ethernet Header | IP Header | TCP Header | HTTP Data | FCS]
```
